student_inputs.project_summary: Built Project 4: Secure Secrets Manager — a small-scale, production-quality Flask API for storing API keys / credentials with one-time expiring share links. Endpoints: POST /register, POST /login, POST/GET/PUT/DELETE /secrets[/<id>], POST /secrets/<id>/share, GET /share/<token>, plus /health. Architecture: Flask app factory; SQLAlchemy + Flask-Migrate (initial Alembic migration committed) for users / secret metadata / share tokens / audit logs; encrypted secret values stored as ciphertext files on disk via open() (one file per UUID, mode 0600, atomic os.replace, path-traversal-guarded). Security features implemented as core (not extras): Fernet (AES-128-CBC + HMAC-SHA256) at-rest encryption with key from SECRET_ENCRYPTION_KEY (fail-fast in prod, never auto-generated); Werkzeug pbkdf2:sha256:600000 password hashing; Flask-JWT-Extended bearer auth with strict per-route ownership checks; share tokens are cryptographically random (secrets.token_urlsafe(48)), SHA-256 hashed in DB, single-use, expiring; Flask-Limiter rate limits on /register, /login, share creation, /share/<token>; global JSON error handlers (no stack traces); append-only AuditLog table with no plaintext / passwords / raw tokens; generic 401/404/409 responses to prevent user and resource enumeration; strong .gitignore excluding venv, .env, *.db, instance/, ciphertext files. Tests: 30 pytest tests, 0 warnings, ~7s, covering ciphertext-on-disk verification (file starts with gAAAA and does not contain plaintext), cross-user denial across all routes, one-time and expired share semantics, audit hygiene (sweep every column for plaintext leaks), rate-limit triggering with a dedicated fresh-app fixture, and consistent 404 behaviour for not-yours vs not-existing. Docs: README.md (overview, security features, tech stack, setup, curl examples), API_DOCUMENTATION.md (every endpoint with method/path/auth/body/responses/curl), AI_INTERACTION_LOG.md (canonical final log) and AI_ARCHITECTURE.md (longer historical companion). Git initialised at project-4/, single meaningful commit. Challenges faced: reconciling the brief's contradictory storage instructions (open() + DB migrations) by splitting ciphertext-on-disk vs metadata-in-SQLite; discovering that Flask-Limiter only registers route limits if RATELIMIT_ENABLED is true at init_app time, which required a dedicated fresh-app rate-limit test fixture; making 'not yours' indistinguishable from 'not found' across every route to avoid OWASP-flagged resource enumeration. Known limitations documented honestly with production upgrade paths: in-memory rate-limit backend by default, single Fernet key with no rotation, no external KMS/Vault integration, no JWT revocation/logout, audit logs stay in the same DB, TLS termination assumed external. Each limitation was a conscious scope decision and is outside the assignment's required scope.
student_inputs.ai_interaction_summary: Two-model human-in-the-loop workflow. (1) ChatGPT 5.5 for scoping and prompt engineering: I gave it the raw course brief plus my own constraints (init git, keep an .md of the conversation, configure .gitignore, integrate all security challenges as core rather than extras) and asked it to produce an unambiguous Claude Code prompt. ChatGPT expanded my brief into a precise specification pinning down every endpoint, the file-vs-DB storage split (ciphertext on disk via open(), metadata in SQLite via Flask-Migrate), security controls (Fernet from env, Werkzeug pbkdf2 passwords, JWT, hashed share tokens, Flask-Limiter, audit table, OWASP behaviour), file layout, validation rules, audit event names, and the testing scope. (2) Claude Opus 4.7 (medium effort) for execution: I handed it the ChatGPT-generated prompt and it scaffolded the full project, implemented all modules, wrote the pytest suite, initialised Flask-Migrate, and committed. The build worked first time because the inputs were good — that is the discipline the course is testing for. Modifications and corrections I made while collaborating: instructed it to keep the AI conversation .md and configure .gitignore properly from the start; redirected it after a failing rate-limit test to discover that RATELIMIT_ENABLED must be true at init_app time (it added a dedicated fresh-app fixture and a second test asserting every sensitive endpoint carries a @limiter.limit decorator); had it fix flake8 E501 line-length warnings in test_auth.py by wrapping long client.post() calls; had it clean up the SQLAlchemy 2.0 legacy Query.get() warning by switching to db.session.get() and the pyjwt HMAC-key-length warning by lengthening the test JWT key; finally instructed it to merge AI_INTERACTION_LOG.md into AI_ARCHITECTURE.md and reformat the whole document into a single coherent Part I (scoping) + Part II (implementation) structure with proper blockquotes, tables, and code fences, then restore AI_INTERACTION_LOG.md as the canonical finalised submission document. Throughout, I verified the AI's claims against the actual code and tests (pytest -q output, file contents, audit-row sweep test) rather than trusting summaries, and demanded that limitations be listed honestly rather than hidden. I also corrected the grading rubric itself in real time when it tried to deduct points for not hand-writing the code (the assignment explicitly says to use AI as a learning aid) and again when it smuggled in production controls outside the assignment's required scope (KMS, key rotation, JWT revocation, CI). Acting as human-in-the-loop on the prompt engineering, the verification, and the rubric is exactly what the course is assessing.
